coredata.dc:title: Achieving high-efficient photocatalytic persulfate-activated degradation of tetracycline via carbon dots modified MIL-101(Fe) octahedrons

Sun, Xinhai
Zhang, Shuai
Shi, Weilong
Guo, Feng
coredata.dc:description:
The synergistic reaction of photocatalysis and advanced oxidation is a valid strategy for the degradation of harmful antibiotic wastewater. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) modified MIL-101(Fe) octahedrons to form CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite photocatalyst was synthesized for visible light–driven photocatalytic/persulfate(PS)-activated tetracycline(TC) degradation. The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra, scavenging experiment and electrochemical analysis were carried out to reveal that the high visible light–driven photocatalytic degradation activity of TC over CDs/MIL-101(Fe) photocatalysts is not only ascribed to the production of free active radicals in the CDs/MIL-101(Fe)/PS system (·OH, ·SO4− , 1O2, h
+ and ·O2 −) but also attributed to the consumption of electrons caused by the PS, which can suppress the recombination of photo-generated carriers as well as strong light scattering and electron trapping effects of CDs. Finally, the possible degradation pathways were proposed by analyzing intermediates via liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry technique. This research presents a rational design conception to construct a CDs/PS-based photocatalysis/advanced oxidation technology with high-efficient degradation activity for the remediation of organic antibiotic pollutant wastewater and for the improvement of carrier transport kinetics of photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Achieving high-efficient photocatalytic persulfate-activated degradation of tetracycline via carbon dots modified MIL-101(Fe) octahedrons

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The synergistic reaction of photocatalysis and advanced oxidation is a valid strategy for the degradation of harmful antibiotic wastewater. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) modified MIL-101(Fe) octahedrons to form CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite photocatalyst was synthesized for visible light–driven photocatalytic/persulfate(PS)-activated tetracycline(TC) degradation. The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra, scavenging experiment and electrochemical analysis were carried out to reveal that the high visible light–driven photocatalytic degradation activity of TC over CDs/MIL-101(Fe) photocatalysts is not only ascribed to the production of free active radicals in the CDs/MIL-101(Fe)/PS system (·OH, ·SO4− , 1O2, h
+ and ·O2 −) but also attributed to the consumption of electrons caused by the PS, which can suppress the recombination of photo-generated carriers as well as strong light scattering and electron trapping effects of CDs. Finally, the possible degradation pathways were proposed by analyzing intermediates via liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry technique. This research presents a rational design conception to construct a CDs/PS-based photocatalysis/advanced oxidation technology with high-efficient degradation activity for the remediation of organic antibiotic pollutant wastewater and for the improvement of carrier transport kinetics of photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Among the most important environmental problems worldwide, the ecotoxicological effects caused by the abuse of antibiotics with strong molecular structure stability in natural aquatic systems have brought many adverse effects on the ecological environment, human health and microbial growth [1–4]. In order to solve the pollution problem of antibiotic wastewater, biological treatment, chemical oxidation and physical adsorption methods are proposed, but they suffer from high energy consumption, high cost, complex operation or secondary pollution [5,6]. Accordingly, it is especially imperative to seek a cost-effective treatment technique to remove the antibiotic from wastewater [7,8]. The advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have aroused great interest because of the efficient generation of active reactive oxygen species (ROSs) with strong oxidizing ability for degrading toxic dyes and antibiotics [9,10], and the combination of photocatalysis and AOPs is considered as an ideal way to degrade antibiotics due to their high efficiency, cost-effectiveness and environmental friendliness [11–13]. Compared to Fenton-like technique, which mainly involves the production of hydroxy radicals (·OH), the photocatalysis/AOPs based on persulfate (PS) activation show more attractiveness due to the generation of sulfate radicals with higher redox potential (2.5–3.5 V), longer life cycle and wider pH adaptation range [14]. Meanwhile, the consumption of electrons caused by the PS can suppress the recombination of photo-generated carriers, which realizes the efficient synergistic degradation of pollutants by photocatalysis and PS activation [15]. Nevertheless, as a representative type of heterogeneous oxidation, the photocatalysis/sulfate radical oxidation suffers from a small contact area and is difficult to control, which makes it desirable to develop an ideal photocatalyst to solve the aforementioned problems.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), an emerging class of porous organic adsorbent materials for 20 years, which are constructed from metal ions/clusters and bridging organic ligands, have a periodic network structure and massive porous crystal frameworks [16]. The organic fraction of cyano groups [17], carboxylates [18], phosphonates [19] or crown ether [20] in MOFs acts as bridges connecting metal ions or clusters, influencing the void size and shape of MOFs, which endowing with high porosity, large specific surface area, tailorable structure and customizable function, thus having high potential in many applications, including sensing, gas storage, separation and catalysis [21,22]. In general, the applications of MOFs in the field of photocatalysis are mainly based on the following two mechanisms [23]: (i) Partial MOFs can act a role of semiconductors to generate charge carriers through the photoexcitation of organic linkers or metal clusters; (ii) These materials can display catalytic activity owing to open metal sites and even catalytically active organic linkers. Among these MOF materials, iron-based MOFs (Fe-MOFs), especially MIL-101 (materials of institute lavoisier-101), possess good performances and have been widely used, which are constructed by hard acid Fe(III) and organic carboxylic acid ligands. They have strong coordination bonds, stable backbones, Fe–O clusters and a narrow band gap, implying greater chemical stability, water stability and photosensitivity [24]. Unfortunately, the pristine Fe-MOFs suffer from rapid recombination of photo-generated carriers during the photocatalytic process, which limits their photocatalytic activity. Thus, it is vital to seek an appropriate means for enhancing the separation efficiency of e −/h
+ and excavate the utilization potential of MIL-101(Fe) towards the long wavelength of the visible region (600–800 nm).

Carbon dots (CDs), a new type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with a nuclear structure generally composed of sp2 hybridized carbon nanocrystals or amorphous carbon [25], are characterized by low toxicity and cost [26], size dependence [27], easy functionalization [28] and up-conversion luminescence [29], and their unique fluorescence properties and ultra-high photo-induced electron transfer capability, which make them exhibit outstanding potential in the field of photocatalysis [30]. In order to further enhance the visible light–driven photocatalytic degradation activity of MIL-101(Fe), it is a practicable research idea to introduce CD materials to form composites to accelerate the combination of charge transfer and photocatalytic reaction to construct an efficient photocatalysis/sulfate radical oxidation system for the treatment of antibiotics.

In this work, we successfully designed and developed a series of CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite photocatalysts with coating CDs on the MIL-101(Fe) octahedrons via a facile solvothermal method for achieving efficient visible light–driven photocatalytic/PS-activated tetracycline (TC) degradation capacity. The as-prepared optimal CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite exhibits a significantly improved photodegradation rate of 82.7% under visible light irradiation. The electron spin resonance (ESR) and liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC-MS) technique were conducted to further discuss the degradation mechanism and the intermediates for the CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite in detail.
Tetracycline (C22H24N2O8), para-phthalic acid (C8H6O4) (A.R.≥99%), FeCl3·6H2O (A.R.≥99%) and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, C3H7NO) (A.R.≥99%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd.

Ginkgo biloba-derived CDs solution was synthesized using a typical hydrothermal method based on the previous report [31]. In brief, 10 g of fresh ginkgo biloba from Jiangsu University of Science and Technology and 200 ml of deionized water were added to a 250 ml reaction vessel and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling, the liquid products obtained from the reaction were centrifuged to form the brown-yellow CDs solution. Finally, for ease of use, the CDs solution was freeze-dried into CDs solid powder.

MIL-101(Fe) octahedrons were synthesized by a simple solvothermal method. Typically, 0.423 g of para-phthalic acid and 1.323 g of FeCl3·6H2O were added to 30 ml of DMF solution and stirred for more than 20 min to ensure intensive mixing. Subsequently, the mixed precursor was transferred to a 50-ml reaction vessel and placed in an oven, and heated at 110 °C for 20 h. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was washed with DMF solution and ethanol to remove impurities and dried in a vacuum drying oven.

The CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite photocatalysts were prepared by the same procedure as the aforementioned MIL-101(Fe) octahedrons; the detailed procedure and synthetic steps are given in Fig. 1 . For a change, when para-phthalic acid and FeCl3·6H2O are dissolved in DMF solution, a certain volume (1, 3 and 5 ml) of CDs solution (1 mg·ml−1) needs to be introduced to the above mixed solution and completely mixed. Afterwards, CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composites with different CDs ratio were obtained by the subsequent solvothermal route, and samples were named as 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe), 3-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) and 5-CDs/MIL-101(Fe), respectively.
Specific test operation steps for photocatalytic performance and other corresponding characterizations are listed in the Supplementary Material.

The crystal structures and surface groups of as-prepared MIL-101(Fe) and CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composites were further characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Fig. 2 (a) shows that all samples have typical characteristic peaks at around 9.08°, 9.66°, 10.36°, 16.52°, 17.92° and 18.90°, corresponds to the (0 0 2), (1 0 0), (1 0 1), (1 0 3), (2 0 2) and (2 1 1) planes of the MIL-101(Fe) structure, respectively [32]. The diffraction peaks of CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composites exhibit similar profiles to pristine MIL-101(Fe), suggesting the original crystal structure of MIL-101(Fe) can be well preserved after the incorporation of CDs [33]. The FT-IR spectra in Fig. 2(b) display that the characteristic peaks of all the samples almost matched the typical vibrational mode of the original MIL-101(Fe), indicating that the introduction of CDs did not change the structural characteristics of MIL-101(Fe) [34]. Typically, the broad peak centered at 3450 cm−1 could be related to the O—H longitudinal stretching vibration of the adsorbed water molecules [35,36]. The narrow peak at 553 cm−1 is corresponded to the stretching vibration of the chelating bond formed by Fe3+and the carboxylic functional group (Fe—O bond), and the weak bands at 1660 and 753 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration peaks of the C=C bond and C—H bond on the benzene ring of MIL-101(Fe), while the absorption peaks at 1601 and 1388 cm−1 are assigned to the symmetrical and anti-symmetric stretching vibration peaks of the C—O bond in the carboxyl group [37–39]. In addition, the strengths of C=O and C=C bonds over CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composites have been significantly enhanced compared to pure MIL-101(Fe), which could be ascribed to the introduction of CDs [40].

The morphology and microstructure of MIL-101(Fe) and CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composites were analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 3 (a) illustrates pure MIL-101(Fe) possesses a regular octahedral morphology with smooth surface and uniform size of around 500 nm [41]. In Fig. 3(b), it shows that the 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite retains the same shape as the pure MIL-101(Fe) after the introduction of CDs [42]. The corresponding TEM images of 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) in Fig. 3(c) and (d) exhibit that the octahedral structure of MIL-101(Fe) is not destroyed and the surface becomes rough after the introduction of CDs [43,44]. Further observation of high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) in Fig. 3(e) presents that the CDs are evenly distributed on the surface of MIL-101(Fe) octahedral morphology, and a significant lattice spacing of 0.21 nm in 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite can be consistent with the (001) crystal planes of carbon [45,46], which demonstrates that CDs were successfully introduced in the CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite system. The high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-SEM) and element mapping images of 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) are provided in Fig. 3(e) and (f), which indicates the available sample contains Fe, O and C elements and evenly distributed. In addition, according to the elemental content obtained by energy dispersion X-ray spectrum (EDS) in Fig. S1 in Supplementary Material the C and O content for 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) has been increased significantly with the loading of CDs in comparison of pristine MIL-101(Fe) [47]. The aforementioned characterization results confirm the initial success of CDs anchoring in the MIL-101(Fe) modification system.

The chemical composition and chemical valence of as-prepared MIL-101(Fe) and 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) were further determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement. From the XPS survey spectra in Fig. 4 (a), it can be observed that the spectra of 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite are composed of three main elements, including carbon (C 1s), iron (Fe 2p) and oxygen (O 1s). As for the high-resolution spectra of Fe 2p in Fig. 4(b), Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 are situated at the binding energy of 711.2 and 725.3 eV, respectively, with a binding energy difference of 14.1 eV, which confirms that Fe is in the 3+ oxidation state in MIL-101(Fe) [48]. Moreover, the Fe 2p3/2 spectrum can be deconvoluted into two peaks with binding energies of 710.9 and 712.4 eV, respectively, which can be attributed to the existence of the Fe2+/Fe3+ oxidation states [49]. In addition to these peaks, the extra peak located at 717.1 eV can be ascribed to the satellite peak (see Table S1). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s over MIL-101(Fe) is presented in Fig. 4(c), and it can be deconvoluted into three peaks with binding energies of 529.8, 531.4 and 532.9 eV (see Table S2), distributing to the metal-oxygen bond (Fe—O), C = O bond and the oxygen in hydroxyls or absorbed water (O—H) [50]. The C 1s spectrum for 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) can be decomposed into three peaks located at 284.4, 286.0 and 288.2 eV are attributed to C—C/C=C, C—O and C=O bonds, respectively (see Fig. 4(d) and Table S3), revealing the existence of CDs in CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite [22]. Compared with pure MIL-101(Fe), high-resolution XPS spectra of each element for 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) are changed little, with only a small offset, indicating a possible chemical interaction and electron migration between MIL-101(Fe) and CDs [51].

For the investigation of optical property and energy band structure diagrams over as-prepared samples, the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy and Mott–Schottky (MS) curves were conducted to explore the effect of the introduction of CDs. In Fig. 5 (a), pure MIL-101(Fe) exhibits a board UV–vis light absorption capacity in the entire range of 520–1000 nm, and a typical absorption edge starting at 519 nm can be found over the pristine MIL-101(Fe), which is in agreement with the previous report [52]. Compared with MIL-101(Fe), the 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite in the visible region widened to 601 nm, clearly indicating the strong light scattering and trapping effects of CDs. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectral conversion provided in Fig. 5(b) determines the band gap (E
g ) values of the as-prepared samples based on the Kubelka–Munk function [53], which can be calculated to be 2.68 and 2.56 eV for MIL-101(Fe) and 1-CDs/MIL-101 (Fe), respectively. MS curves were analyzed at three different frequencies of 500, 800 and 1000 Hz (compared to the Ag/AgCl reference electrode) to achieve the positions of the conduction band (CB) for as-prepared samples (Fig. 5(c)). The CB potentials of MIL-101(Fe) and 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) are measured to be −0.21 and −0.26 eV after conversion (E(NHE) = E(Ag/AgCl)+0.197), respectively. Thus, the energy band structure diagrams of MIL-101(Fe) and 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) are provided in Fig. 5(d).

The photocatalytic degradation performance and its capacity to activate PS of the prepared MIL-101(Fe) and CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composites were measured under visible light illumination (λ > 420 nm) by performing the TC degradation, as exhibited in Fig. 6 . All the reaction solution in the presence of catalysts was stirred in the dark for 30 min to reach the adsorption–desorption equilibrium before illumination [54,55]. It is clearly observed in Fig. 6(a) that the concentration of TC solution remains constant after 120 min without the addition of photocatalysts under visible light owing to the stability of TC molecules in the aquatic environment [56]. In the existence of PS without/with visible light conditions, only 7% or 17% of TC was removed after 120 min reaction, which can be due to the photothermal effect generated by light irradiation [57]. Notably, the visible light–driven degradation activity was significantly enhanced after the introduction of MIL-101(Fe), which is in virtue of the fact that photo-generated electrons can be excited to react with O2 to produce active species with strong oxidative properties [58]. Based on this, after the addition of PS, the degradation efficiency of MIL-101(Fe) was significantly enhanced due to the activation of PS by MIL-101(Fe) to produce ·SO4 and ·OH [38,59]. Furthermore, since the electronic capturing effect of CDs [60], the photocatalytic activities of CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite were further improved with the loading of CDs under visible light irradiation. The corresponding pseudo-first-order kinetic curves in different systems were exhibited in Fig. 6(b), and fitted degradation rate constants of k (min−1) are displayed in Table S4. It is clearly seen that the optimal 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite (0.00512 min−1) is 1.59 times higher than that of the pure MIL-101(Fe) (0.00323 min−1), while the optimal kinetic reaction rate of 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) with additional PS (0.01451 min−1) is 2.83 times higher than that of 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) in the absence of PS under visible light condition (Fig. 6(b)), illustrating that the advanced performance of the photocatalytic degradation driven by visible light is mainly the electron capture effect of CDs and the PS activation process of MIL-101(Fe) [61]. Comparison of the photocatalytic performance of the MIL-101(Fe)/PS reaction system with other previously reported PS-based TC degradation reaction systems (Table S5) shows that under the same conditions, the MIL-101(Fe)/PS system has the lowest concentration of catalyst/PS and the highest concentration of degraded TC [62]. The stability of the as-prepared 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) photocatalyst was evaluated via cycle stability experiments, and the results demonstrating that the degradation rate of TC expresses no significant decrease after five cycles (Fig. 6(c)). Finally, the XRD pattern (Fig. 6(d)) and SEM image (Fig. S2) of the used 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite after photocatalytic degradation reaction represented that there was little difference in microscopic topography and the crystal structure, further indicating the excellent stability of the CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite [63]. After the completion of the photocatalytic reaction, Fe ions leached from the optimal samples were measured with an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Agilent 5110) (Table S6). It can be found that only a very small amount of Fe ions is leached out, which implies that the CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite photocatalysts are very stable as well as environmentally compatible [64].

For the investigation of the relaxation process of photo-generated charge carriers, photoluminescence (PL) spectra, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photocurrent spectra of as-prepared samples were carried out [65]. As exhibited in Fig. 7 (a), the primary PL emission peaks of MIL-101(Fe) and 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composites approximately locate at 467 nm, and the intensity of PL spectrum for 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) represented a palpable reduction with the introduction of CDs compared with MIL-101(Fe), testifying that the surface separation capacity of electron–hole pairs can effectively ameliorated owing to the electron-transfer property of CDs [66]. Moreover, from EIS plots in Fig. 7(b), the impedance arc radius of 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) is also far smaller than that of MIL-101(Fe), which further demonstrates the high electrical conductivity and rapid interfacial charge transfer activity of the CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite [67]. The results of photocurrent response (Fig. 7(c) and (d)) reveal that the photocurrent intensity and cycling are relatively stable in all samples, and the 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite (0.12 μA·cm−2) exhibits significantly higher photocurrent intensity than MIL-101(Fe) (0.022 μA·cm−2), indicating loading CDs could effectively enhance the charge transport and relaxation dynamics of MIL-101(Fe) [68]. To further research the photoexcited carriers’ transfer efficiency through adding PS to Na2SO4 electrolyte solution, the photocurrent intensities of MIL-101(Fe) and 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) increase to 0.032 and 0.18 μA·cm−2, respectively, which implies the important role of PS activation in the rapid charge carriers transfer over CDs/MIL-101(Fe) degradation system [64].

In order to probe the main role of different types and quality of free active species in 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite and the TC degradation mechanism of combining photocatalysis and PS activation [69], the capturing experiments and ESR spectra of free radicals were carried out. Before the degradation reaction, the scavengers of vitamin C (VC), tert-butanol (TBA), L-tryptophan (LTP), methanol (MeOH) and triethanolamine (TEOA) were added into the TC solution for testing the effects of ·O2 −, ·OH, 1O2, ·SO4− and h
+ , respectively [70]. As exhibited in Fig. 8 (a) and (b), the final degradation rates of TC were effectively inhibited after adding MeOH, TBA, LTP and TEOA, and decreased to 54.2%, 52.2%, 37.6% and 19.3%, respectively, which indicates that ·SO4− , ·OH, 1O2 and h
+ are the main free active radicals in the 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe)/PS system. The degradation rate has decreased slightly after the introduction of VC, indicating that ·O2 − is not the main ROS during the degradation process [71]. ESR spectra have been performed to further detect the ·OH, ·O2 − and ·SO4− with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the quenched agent [72], as provided in Fig. 8(c) and (d). There is no characteristic signal of DMPO-·OH, DMPO-·O2 − and DMPO-·SO4− under dark conditions, while after 10 min of visible irradiation, the strong signals of DMPO-·OH, DMPO-·O2 − and DMPO-·SO4 − can be observed, confirming the existence of ·OH, 1O2, ·O2 − and ·SO4 − in the activated PS degradation process over the 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite system.

As exhibited in Fig. 9 , the possible mechanism of the enhanced degradation activity for CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite can be well expounded under visible light illumination and PS activation by the above experimental results and analysis. Under the visible light irradiation, MIL-101(Fe) can be excited to produce photo-generated electron-hole pairs (e −−h
+ ) (Eq. (1)), which can be separated and transferred to the CB and valence band (VB) [73], respectively. Significantly, e − are extracted onto the surface of CDs owing to the electron-capturing feature [74], which could promote the separation and transfer of electron–hole pairs of MIL-101(Fe), consistent with the results of the photoluminescence (PL) spectra in Fig. 7(a). Meanwhile, the e − at the CB could oxidize O2 to generate superoxide radicals (·O2 −) directly (Eq. (2)) since the CB position of CDs/MIL-101(Fe) (−0.46 eV) is more negative than the transition potential between O2 and ·O2 − (−0.33 eV) [75]. Nevertheless, compared with MIL-101(Fe) (VB = 2.47 eV, h
+ + H2O → ·OH + H+), the position (2.3 eV) of VB for CDs/MIL-101(Fe) is negative than conversion potential between ·OH and H2O (2.4 eV), thus h
+ on VB cannot react with H2O to produce hydroxyl radicals (·OH), but further degrades TC to form CO2 and H2O due to the strong oxidizing property of h
+ [76,77]. In addition, the PS activation process could promote the generation of more free radicals to degrade pollutants: one is the formation of sulfate radicals (·SO4 −) via recombining e
− with PS (Eq. (3)), further facilitating the separation of electron-hole pairs; other is the formation of ·OH by recombining ·SO4 − with H2O (Eq. (4)) [78]. The formation of 1O2 is accompanied by the depletion of ·O2 − (Eq. (5)). Finally, ·OH, h
+ , 1O2, ·O2 − and ·SO4 − could degrade TC into H2O and CO2, etc.
(1) CDs/MIL-101(Fe) + hv→ e
− + h
+
(2)
e
− + O2 → ·O2 −
(3) S2O8 2− + e
− → ·SO4 − + SO4 2−
(4) ·SO4 − + H2O → ·OH + SO4 2− + H+
(5) 2·O2 − + 2H+→1O2 + H2O2

The possible transformation pathways and the mass-to-charge ratio (m/z) and molecular structures of the major intermediates produced during the process of photocatalytic degradation for TC in the Vis/CDs/MIL-101(Fe)/PS system were further investigated via LC-MS technique, and the corresponding spectra are displayed in Fig. S3. The main signals at m/z of 445 corresponding to the molecular formula of TC (C22H24N2O8) gradually decreased during the degradation process, and other signals ascribed to smaller molecular compounds were formed, indicating that TC was effectively decomposed and catalyzed by the synergy of photocatalysis and PS activation over the CDs/MIL-101(Fe) sample because functional groups containing double bonds, such as phenols and amino groups, are relatively high in TC and are therefore susceptible to reactive species. The intermediates are mainly produced through two modes: the loss or cleavage of specific functional groups and ring-opening reactions, two possible degradation pathways of TC were thus proposed and exhibited in Fig. 10 . In the first place, product 1 (P1, m/z = 417) was formed via breaking N—CH3 and C—NH2 [79], and further dividing into two degradative paths. For the pathway 1, product 1 (P1, m/z = 417) losing a water molecule via the dehydration reaction, resulting in product 2 (P2, m/z = 399), and further converted into P3 (m/z = 228) owing to breaking of C—C/C=C bond [80]. For the pathway 2, P4 (m/z = 340) can be formed by losing the group of —C=O and N-demethylation (losing N—CH3) from P1 owing to the low energy of N—C bond [81]. Then, P5 (m/z = 271) was formed through the dehydration reaction [82], and further forming P3 via losing the group of —C=O. Afterwards, P3 is attacked by free radicals, and P6 (m/z = 192), P7 (m/z = 60) and P8 (m/z = 74) were formed by continuous open-ring reaction. Ultimately, a part of the above intermediate could be completely decomposed into CO2 and H2O by the collaborative efforts of active species.

In summary, a series of CDs/MIL-101(Fe) composite photocatalysts with coating CDs on the MIL-101(Fe) octahedrons were synthesized successfully via a facile solvothermal method for achieving efficient visible light–driven photocatalytic/PS-activated TC degradation capacity. The optimal 1-CDs/MIL-101(Fe) sample exhibits a significantly enhanced photocatalytic TC degradation rate of 82.7% with the addition of PS under visible light irradiation. A series of characterization results indicate that the significant improvement in degradation performance is mainly due to the cooperative activation of PS by the combination of CDs and photo-induced electrons that are produced from MIL-101(Fe), which not only improves the optical absorption of MIL-101(Fe) as well as the electron relaxation ability but also exploits the electron transport properties of CDs, producing free active radicals in the CDs/MIL-101(Fe)/PS system (·OH, ·SO4 −, h
+ and ·O2 −). This study is expected to provide an efficient design to achieve the combination of advanced oxidation and photocatalysis for alleviating the harm of antibiotic pollution to the water environment.
